```{r recommended-manuscript-tables}
  "Table 3", "function-inventory", "Main text", "Condensed function-family counts; move full function list to supplement.",
  "Table 4", "pupil-models and aoi-window-models", "Supplement", "Object-status table showing which modelling workflows completed successfully.",
  "Table 5", "reporting-readiness-exclusions", "Supplement", "Readiness, exclusion, checklist, and decision-audit object summary.",
  "Table S1", "function-inventory", "Supplement", "Complete exported-function inventory.",
  "Table S2", "reproducibility", "Supplement", "Session information and package versions."
)

recommended_tables %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

```{r generated-paper-figure-files}
tibble(
  figure_file = list.files(paper_figures_dir, pattern = "\\.png$", full.names = FALSE)
) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```


# 20. Discussion

This paper-only case study demonstrates `gp3tools` as a workflow-oriented R package for Gazepoint GP3 and Gazepoint Analysis exports. The package addresses a practical gap that often appears between data collection and statistical modelling: researchers receive multiple proprietary CSV exports, but still need to verify file pairing, sampling rate, gaze validity, pupil availability, AOI coding, fixation structure, preprocessing choices, exclusion decisions, and reporting completeness before fitting confirmatory or exploratory models. The demonstration shows that these steps can be organised into a reproducible R workflow rather than handled through disconnected manual checks.

The first contribution of `gp3tools` is structured import and diagnostic processing. The paper-only synthetic dataset contained 48 complete all-gaze/fixation file pairs, 115,200 all-gaze rows, 5,691 fixation rows, 192 recording-level sampling and quality rows, and 1,344 AOI summary rows. The high-level workflow identified complete file pairs, estimated the nominal 60 Hz sampling rate, produced quality-control outputs, flagged review-required recordings, generated AOI summaries, wrote CSV files, saved diagnostic plots, and produced an HTML report. This supports the package’s role as a first-pass inspection framework for Gazepoint export folders.

The second contribution is analysis-ready data preparation. The workflow converted Gazepoint-style exports into harmonised all-gaze, fixation, master-table, pupil-window, AOI-window, fixation-summary, transition, and adapter objects. This is important because many downstream models require consistent subject, trial, time, condition, pupil, gaze-coordinate, AOI, and validity columns. By standardising these objects, `gp3tools` reduces the need for repeated project-specific data wrangling and makes the path from exported rows to model-ready data more transparent.

The third contribution is transparent preprocessing and sensitivity analysis. The pupil workflow demonstrates raw, interpolated, PCHIP, baseline-corrected, smoothed, Hampel-filtered, and audit-ready branches. The AOI workflow demonstrates denominator checks, empirical-logit transformations, binomial GLMM preparation, AOI time-course GAMM preparation, and transition summaries. The model workflow demonstrates nested-model comparison, model summaries, leave-one-unit checks, prediction plotting, divergence-point estimation, and optional multiverse-style preprocessing summaries. These features support transparent reporting because they preserve preprocessing and modelling decisions as explicit objects rather than hidden script side effects.

The fourth contribution is reporting readiness. The workflow creates exclusion recommendations, readiness gates, reporting checklists, condition-quality audits, post-exclusion balance checks, and final analysis-decision audits. These outputs are especially useful for applied eye-tracking research, where interpretation should depend not only on model coefficients but also on data quality, AOI denominator adequacy, missingness patterns, exclusion rules, and diagnostic status. The package does not automatically decide which data should be removed or which model should be preferred. Instead, it provides structured evidence so that researchers can make and report these decisions explicitly.

The synthetic demonstration also clarifies the intended interpretation of package outputs. Some numerical patterns in the paper-only dataset were deliberately created to make the workflow visible: early-target and late-target trials show different pupil and target-looking trajectories, the distractor-heavy condition contains more distractor viewing and more review-required rows, and the fixation files contain enough events to support fixation and transition summaries. These patterns are useful for demonstrating software functionality, but they are not empirical evidence for psychological, marketing, or perceptual effects. The appropriate inference is that `gp3tools` can process and report a realistic Gazepoint-like workflow end to end.

For applied researchers, the main value of `gp3tools` is practical reproducibility. A typical Gazepoint project can start with `run_gazepoint_workflow()` for folder-level inspection, then move to master-table validation, pupil preprocessing, AOI-window summaries, fixation and transition summaries, model-ready tables, diagnostics, sensitivity checks, exclusion recommendations, and reporting checklists. This workflow is flexible enough to support lightweight teaching examples, private real-export smoke tests, and more comprehensive paper-style demonstrations such as the one shown here.

# 21. Limitations

The main limitation of this case study is that the demonstration dataset is synthetic. The data were designed to mimic a realistic Gazepoint export folder and to exercise package functionality, but they do not represent observations from real participants. Therefore, the condition differences shown in pupil traces, AOI viewed time, target-looking trajectories, fixation counts, transition probabilities, or model summaries should not be interpreted as substantive findings. They are demonstration patterns created to test whether the software workflow can produce meaningful objects, figures, and reports.

A second limitation is that synthetic data can only approximate the complexity of real eye-tracking data. Real Gazepoint exports may contain calibration problems, participant movement, unusual timing structures, inconsistent AOI labels, missing files, duplicate files, different screen setups, task interruptions, nonstandard summary exports, stimulus-specific brightness differences, or experiment-specific event markers. The paper-only generator includes invalid samples, missing pupil intervals, out-of-range gaze coordinates, participant-level quality variation, fixation files, and drift, but it cannot cover every possible real-world export structure.

A third limitation is that some advanced branches depend on optional packages or on study-specific design information. For example, external `gazer` or `eyetools` checks require the relevant packages and compatible input structures. Stimulus-luminance auditing requires stimulus image files. Gaze recalibration requires known target or check-target coordinates. Estimated marginal means require `emmeans`. Negative-binomial transition-count sensitivity requires `glmmTMB`. The package is designed to skip, report, or return controlled status information when optional components are unavailable, but users still need to understand which branches are appropriate for their study.

A fourth limitation concerns statistical interpretation. `gp3tools` prepares model-ready data and provides representative wrappers for LMMs, GLMMs, GAMMs, GCA-style models, cluster-preparation workflows, divergence-point estimation, and sensitivity checks. These tools do not remove the need for careful model specification, preregistration where appropriate, theoretical justification of time windows and AOIs, inspection of assumptions, and transparent reporting of exploratory decisions. The package can make the workflow more reproducible, but it cannot determine the correct scientific model automatically.

A fifth limitation is that the paper-only R Markdown file is intentionally comprehensive. It demonstrates many package families in one document, which is useful for a software paper and supplementary material, but a normal empirical article should use a narrower workflow aligned with its hypotheses. In practice, researchers should select the import, quality-control, preprocessing, AOI, pupil, fixation, transition, modelling, and reporting helpers that match their study design rather than treating every function as mandatory.

Finally, the package has so far been validated through automated tests, synthetic example datasets, a lightweight GitHub demonstration dataset, this larger paper-only synthetic showcase, and private real-export smoke tests. Broader validation on independent real Gazepoint datasets from different laboratories, tasks, stimulus types, and participant populations would strengthen confidence in edge-case handling and interoperability.

# 22. Data and code availability

The `gp3tools` package is publicly available from the project GitHub repository. The package repository contains the source code, documentation, README workflow guidance, vignettes, tests, example datasets, and a lightweight synthetic Gazepoint-style demonstration export folder suitable for package examples and GitHub-based use.

The larger synthetic dataset used in this paper-only R Markdown workflow was generated outside the package repository to avoid increasing package size. It is stored locally under the paper-showcase folder and contains synthetic all-gaze files, synthetic fixation files, and a synthetic summary export. The dataset was designed for manuscript demonstration and does not contain real participant data.

The generator script for the paper-only synthetic dataset is stored as:

```text
C:/Users/Stefanos-PC/Desktop/gp3tools_paper_showcase/01_create_paper_synthetic_gazepoint_exports.R
```

The paper-only R Markdown file is stored as:

```text
C:/Users/Stefanos-PC/Desktop/gp3tools_paper_showcase/paper_rmd/02_gp3tools_paper_showcase.Rmd
```

The rendered HTML output is stored as:

```text
C:/Users/Stefanos-PC/Desktop/gp3tools_paper_showcase/rendered_html/02_gp3tools_paper_showcase.html
```

The compact manuscript-style figures generated by the R Markdown workflow are stored in:

```text
C:/Users/Stefanos-PC/Desktop/gp3tools_paper_showcase/figures
```

For journal submission, the recommended availability statement is:

> The `gp3tools` source code is available from the project GitHub repository. The paper demonstration uses a synthetic Gazepoint-like export dataset generated for software-validation and illustration purposes. No real participant data are included in the paper-only synthetic dataset. The synthetic data generator, paper R Markdown workflow, rendered HTML report, and selected output tables/figures can be provided as supplementary material or deposited in an archival repository such as OSF or Zenodo.

If the paper-only synthetic dataset is later shared publicly, it should be shared as supplementary material, an OSF component, a Zenodo archive, or a GitHub release asset rather than committed directly into the R package source repository. This keeps the package lightweight while preserving reproducibility for the manuscript.

# 23. Practical implications

`gp3tools` has practical implications for researchers using Gazepoint GP3 or Gazepoint Analysis exports. First, it reduces the amount of custom import and cleaning code needed at the start of a project. Researchers can inspect file pairing, sampling rate, tracking quality, AOI summaries, and standard outputs before moving into more specialised analyses.

Second, the package encourages transparent preprocessing. Pupil preprocessing, interpolation, baseline correction, smoothing, gap auditing, quality imbalance checks, and sensitivity branches can be stored as explicit R objects. This makes it easier to describe preprocessing choices in manuscripts and to evaluate whether conclusions depend on those choices.

Third, the package supports both confirmatory and exploratory workflows. Confirmatory analyses can be based on predefined pupil windows or AOI windows using LMM and GLMM workflows. Exploratory or complementary analyses can use pupil GAMMs, AOI GAMMs, transition matrices, sequence-preparation objects, divergence-point estimation, cluster-preparation utilities, and leave-one-unit sensitivity checks. This separation helps researchers avoid mixing exploratory window discovery with confirmatory hypothesis testing.

Fourth, the package supports reporting discipline. Readiness gates, exclusion recommendations, reporting checklists, model diagnostics, and analysis-decision audits can be used before final interpretation. These objects help researchers document why data were retained or reviewed, which models were fitted, which diagnostics were inspected, and which outputs are suitable for manuscript reporting.

Fifth, `gp3tools` can support teaching and supervision. Because it uses exported CSV files and synthetic examples, students can learn a complete eye-tracking workflow without needing access to private participant data. The paper-only synthetic dataset is especially useful for demonstrating how quality-control, preprocessing, modelling, and reporting components connect in a realistic analysis pipeline.

# 24. Conclusion

This paper-only case study shows that `gp3tools` can support a complete and transparent Gazepoint GP3 analysis workflow in R. Starting from a realistic synthetic export folder, the package imported all-gaze and fixation files, verified file pairing, estimated sampling rate, summarised tracking quality, flagged recordings requiring review, created analysis-ready tables, audited master data, demonstrated gaze recalibration, summarised AOI viewing, prepared pupil and AOI model data, ran representative model and sensitivity workflows, created fixation and transition summaries, prepared interoperability outputs, and generated reporting-readiness objects.

The demonstration supports the central software contribution of `gp3tools`: it turns Gazepoint export folders into reproducible, inspectable, model-ready, and report-ready R objects. The package is not a substitute for experimental design, calibration quality, theoretical modelling, or statistical judgement. Instead, it provides the workflow infrastructure needed to make those decisions transparent.

The results also show why a richer paper-only synthetic dataset is useful for software demonstration. A lightweight GitHub dataset is appropriate for package examples and quick checks, but a larger synthetic export folder can better exercise advanced workflows, produce clearer figures, and support manuscript-style explanation without exposing private participant data.

Overall, `gp3tools` offers a practical bridge between Gazepoint data export and reproducible R-based eye-tracking analysis. Its main value lies in workflow transparency: researchers can see how files were imported, how quality was assessed, how preprocessing was performed, how AOI and pupil outcomes were prepared, how models and sensitivity checks were run, and how reporting decisions were documented.





# 25. Reproducibility information

```{r reproducibility}
sessionInfo()
```
